failed.title: Minimum interest floor with put right

FAIL if there is no minimum floor or no put right at the floor.

failed.title: Non-compete product scope includes pipeline products meeting R&D-spend threshold
failed.match: PASS if the product scope includes pipeline products that meet a defined R&D-spend threshold (not just products already sold at exit). FAIL if pipeline products are excluded entirely or included without any qualifying threshold.

failed.title: Non-compete includes product-by-product geographic narrowing mechanism
failed.match: PASS if the provision includes a product-by-product narrowing mechanism that limits geography for product lines sold in fewer than all three North American countries (a concession to Kastor). FAIL if the North America default applies uniformly to all products without narrowing.

failed.title: Buy-sell includes shoot-out/Russian roulette mechanic
failed.match: PASS if the buy-sell provision includes a shoot-out (Russian roulette) mechanic where the triggering party names a price and the receiving party may elect to buy or sell at that price, providing bilateral price discipline. FAIL if no such mechanic is included.

failed.title: Third-party sublicensing requires other member's consent (not unreasonably withheld)
failed.match: PASS if sublicenses to third parties (non-affiliates) require the other member's consent, with such consent not to be unreasonably withheld. FAIL if third-party sublicensing is freely permitted or absolutely prohibited without a consent mechanism.

# Deliverable Summary: MKPoly JVA Compromise Provisions

## Output File
**`mkpoly-jva-compromise-provisions.docx`** — validated Microsoft Word document (ECMA-376 schema-valid, 2 tables, full heading structure).

## Source Documents Reviewed (14 total)
All documents in `/workspace/documents` were read in full:
- **Term sheet** (executed, March 15, 2025) and **JVA v.3 (clean)** and **JVA v.4 (redline tracked)** — the operative drafts and the parties' competing markups.
- **Kastor position summary** (Escalante, Aug 1) and **Meridian negotiation playbook** (Falk, Aug 1) — each side's positions, fallback ladders, minimum acceptable terms, and priority rankings for the five contested provisions.
- **Three negotiation emails** (Falk→Escalante Jul 28; Escalante→Falk Jul 30; Cordero→Falk Jul 31) — confirming the five open points, the fallback-ladder process, the deliverable format (clause language + rationale, independently evaluable), and each principal's authorized flexibility.
- **Non-compete enforceability memo** (Falk, Aug 4) — multi-jurisdictional survey (DE/TX/NY/CA) supporting the 18-month / hybrid-geography / substantially-similar-scope compromise.
- **Frazier Whitmore valuation benchmarks** (xlsx) — comparable transactions (6.2×–8.1× range, 7.1× median), EBITDA multiple analysis, and DCF-vs.-multiple comparison.
- **Capital contribution schedule** and **IP schedule** (xlsx) — contribution-to-equity asymmetry ($43.8M = 50.5% but 45% equity) and IP asymmetry (23 Meridian items @ $28.6M vs. 9 Kastor items not independently valued).
- **Standard JVA template** — used as the pandoc reference document for legal styling.

## The Five Contested Provisions and Compromise Landing Points

| # | Provision (JVA location) | Compromise |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Management Committee & Voting (Art. IV) | 3-2 board (Meridian majority) + supermajority 4-of-5 (incl. ≥1 Kastor) on 12 Reserved Matters + tiered deadlock (CEO casting vote on operational matters; forced buyout on Reserved Matters) |
| 2 | Capital Calls (Art. V) | 45-day funding + 30-day cure (75 days total); penalty interest (greater of SOFR+400bps or 9%); Cure Loan (secured, auto-converts, single-use/24mo); 15% annual dilution cap with forced-sale backstop; EUR funding accommodation |
| 3 | Non-Compete (Art. XIV) | 18 months; JV products + substantially similar/directly competitive; North America; 12-month non-solicit |
| 4 | Exit Valuation (Art. X) | 7.0× trailing-12-mo EBITDA anchor; independent appraiser (not Frazier Whitmore) with mutual-agreement/AAA selection; baseball arbitration; 6.5×–7.5× collar; EBITDA primary; no discounts; 4 triggers; equal cost-sharing |
| 5 | IP License-Back (Art. XII) | Contributed IP reverts + 12-mo transitional license, no ongoing cross-license; co-developed IP non-exclusive/royalty-free/perpetual to BOTH; territorial sublicensing (Meridian: Americas; Kastor: RoW); reciprocal Restricted Competitors covenant + 25% NA-revenue negative covenant + audit right; improvements revert to contributor with restricted license to other party |

## Document Structure
- **Section I** — Executive Summary (purpose, process, overall balance, foundational economic context)
- **Section II** — Summary Concession Matrix (6-column table: Meridian opening / Kastor opening / compromise / Meridian gains-concedes / Kastor gains-concedes, for all 5 provisions)
- **Sections III–VII** — One self-contained section per contested provision, each containing: (A) summary of both parties' positions; (B) ready-to-insert clause language; (C) negotiation rationale explaining concessions and gains for both sides; (D) cross-references to related JVA provisions and schedules
- **Section VIII** — Overall Balance Assessment (net assessment for each party, red-line confirmation, cumulative concession check)

## Completeness Audit (performed after first draft)

A mandatory completeness audit was run against the source record. The audit re-listed every distinct issue/requirement the source documents raise and cross-checked the deliverable by content. The following gaps were identified and filled (each is a distinct, record-supported point that the first draft had omitted or only implied):

1. **Forum-selection clause (Provision 3)** — the enforceability memo recommends a *mandatory* forum-selection clause directing non-compete enforcement to the Delaware Court of Chancery or Harris County, Texas courts. First draft mentioned it only in rationale; **added as ready-to-insert clause language** (new Section 14.04(b)).
2. **Early-exit scaling provision (Provision 3)** — the memo recommends scaling the restraint to 12 months for exits within the first 24 months after formation (protectable interest is commensurate with JV operating duration). **Added as clause language** (new Section 14.04(a)).
3. **Hybrid revenue-threshold geography (Provision 3)** — the memo specifically recommends "US, Canada, + any jurisdiction where MKPoly generated >$500,000 in the 24 months preceding exit" to satisfy California §16602.5. **Added as an optional refinement** (Section 14.04(d)) the parties may adopt.
4. **Choice-of-law confirmation (Provision 3)** — memo recommends confirming Delaware law governs the non-compete. **Added** (Section 14.04(c)).
5. **Case authority (Provision 3 rationale)** — added the specific supporting citations the memo provides: *Peat Marwick v. Haass* (18-mo, TX), *Boulden v. Stilwell* (18-mo LLC, DE), *Marsh USA v. Cook* (2-yr, TX), *EDIX Media v. Mahani* (2-yr, DE), *Edwards v. Arthur Andersen* (CA voids), *Fillpoint v. Maas* and *Kelton v. Stravinski* (CA exceptions).
6. **Reformation/blue-pencil doctrines by jurisdiction (Provision 3 rationale)** — added the jurisdiction-specific differences (DE discretionary modification; TX mandatory §15.51 with §15.51(c) fee-shifting; NY blue-pencil; CA no reformation/void entirely).
7. **California preemptive declaratory-judgment risk (Provision 3 rationale)** — added the memo's specific risk that a member could file a preemptive CA action and the forum-selection clause mitigates but does not eliminate this.
8. **Restricted Competitors list discrepancy (Provision 5)** — flagged that the v.4 draft Schedule 12.05 lists global coatings majors (PPG, Sherwin-Williams, Akzo Nobel, etc.) while a separately proposed list identifies North American specialists (Polaris, Endurance, Trident, etc.); these must be reconciled by the principals. **Added as an explicit open item** in both Provision 3 and Provision 5 cross-references.
9. **Implied dollar premium figure (foundational context)** — added the specific $4,785,000 figure from the capital contribution schedule Note 1 (Kastor contributes 50.52% of capital but holds 45%).

After the additions, the document was regenerated and re-validated (ECMA-376 schema-valid). A final term scan confirmed all previously-missing terms are now present, and all five provisions retain their four required components (Summary of Positions / Clause Language / Negotiation Rationale / Cross-References).

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

**D. California.** California **does not apply the blue-pencil doctrine** to non-competition covenants that fall outside the statutory exceptions (Sections 16601--16602.5). If a non-compete does not qualify under the LLC/partnership exception or another statutory exception, it is **void in its entirety** --- a California court will not narrow it to save it. The California Supreme Court\'s decision in *Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP* made this point emphatically, holding that Section 16600 \"protects Californians and ensures \'that every citizen shall retain the right to pursue any lawful employment and enterprise of their choice,\'\" and that courts may not circumvent this protection by reforming an otherwise void covenant into one that falls within a statutory exception.

This all-or-nothing approach makes California the most hostile jurisdiction for non-competition covenants and underscores the critical importance of ensuring that the covenant, as drafted, satisfies the Section 16602.5 requirements on its face rather than relying on judicial rescue.

**Practical Recommendation.** We recommend including a **severability and reformation clause** in Section 11.01 of the MKPoly JVA that expressly authorizes a court of competent jurisdiction to reform the covenant to the minimum extent necessary to render it enforceable under applicable law. While this clause will not be effective in a California court (which would disregard it under *Edwards*), it will be effective in Delaware, Texas, and New York --- the jurisdictions most likely to adjudicate enforceability.

To mitigate the California risk, the severability and reformation clause should be paired with a **forum-selection clause** directing enforcement actions under Section 11.01 to the Delaware Court of Chancery or, in the alternative, the federal or state courts of Harris County, Texas. This forum-selection clause will channel enforcement litigation to jurisdictions where reformation is available and where the covenant is most likely to be enforced as drafted. We discuss this recommendation further in Section VIII.B below.

> For a period of twelve (12) months following the effective date of a Member's exit from the Company by any means (whether through buyout, dissolution, Transfer, or otherwise), neither the exiting Member nor any of its Affiliates shall, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment, recruit, hire, or otherwise engage (whether as an employee, independent contractor, consultant, or otherwise) any individual who was an employee of the Company at any time during the twelve (12) months immediately preceding the effective date of such Member's exit, without the prior written consent of the remaining Member or the Company. The foregoing restriction shall not apply to (i) general solicitations of employment not specifically targeted at employees of the Company, including advertisements in newspapers, trade publications, or online job boards, provided that the exiting Member or its Affiliates do not hire any employee of the Company who responds to such general solicitation without the prior written consent of the remaining Member or the Company, or (ii) any employee of the Company who was terminated by the Company prior to the date of solicitation by the exiting Member or its Affiliates.

### C. Negotiation Rationale

**Why this is a fair middle ground.** The compromise lands each of the three reasonableness dimensions — duration, product scope, and geography — at a point supported by both the parties' stated flexibility and the enforceability analysis across the jurisdictions most likely to govern MKPoly (Delaware and Texas, with New York and California as secondary considerations).

I re-read the key source documents (the non-compete enforceability memo, the capital contribution schedule, the v.4 draft Schedule 12.05, and the Meridian playbook's proposed Restricted Competitors list) and cross-checked every distinct point against the deliverable by opening the output file and scanning its content.
